deduplication_summary.note: Some TECH2 hypothesis IDs (H003, H004, H006, H007) appear twice in the input with different content. When such an ID appears in both threats_to_remove and unique_threats_retained, one variant is removed and one kept—see group descriptions for disambiguation.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: PermissionValidator out-of-bounds calldata read via LibBytes.readBytes32 allows session key holders to bypass parameter permission rules by crafting short call.data that causes calldataload to read adjacent attacker-controlled calldata
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All seven threats describe the identical code defect: LibBytes.readBytes32 uses calldataload without bounds checking in PermissionValidator.validatePermission, creating a validation-time vs execution-time data divergence that bypasses session permission rules. Same root cause file (PermissionValidator.sol + LibBytes.sol), same exploitation mechanism, same impact.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Unchecked uint256 weight overflow in BaseSig.recoverBranch when FLAG_SUBDIGEST sets weight to type(uint256).max and subsequent signer weight additions wrap to small values, causing DoS on subdigest-based authentication
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All ten threats describe the same arithmetic overflow: unchecked block in recoverBranch allows weight = type(uint256).max + addrWeight to wrap to near-zero, failing threshold check. Same function (BaseSig.recoverBranch), same mechanism, same DoS impact. Note: TECH2-H007 refers to the 'Unchecked Weight Overflow with FLAG_SUBDIGEST' variant; TECH2-H003 refers to the 'Unchecked weight overflow after SUBDIGEST/ANY_ADDRESS_SUBDIGEST' variant (not the Cross-Wallet Replay attestation variant).

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: ecrecover returning address(0) on invalid signatures unconditionally contributes weight to the signature tree in BaseSig.recoverBranch, enabling wallet takeover if address(0) is misconfigured as a signer
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All seven threats describe the identical missing validation: BaseSig.recoverBranch does not check ecrecover result for address(0) before adding weight and computing a leaf. Same function, same code defect, same config-dependent wallet takeover impact.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: SessionSig blacklist sort validation uses strict less-than instead of less-than-or-equal, allowing duplicate consecutive addresses in the blacklist
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All three threats describe the same off-by-one in SessionSig.recoverConfiguration blacklist sorting (< instead of <=). Same function, same code pattern, same impact on imageHash computation and blacklist integrity.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Recovery.sol provides no mechanism to cancel queued payloads, creating an irrevocable time-bomb after recovery signer key compromise
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All four threats describe the same design gap: Recovery.queuePayload stores timestamps permanently with no cancel/remove function, forcing a race condition between attacker execution and owner config update after signer compromise. Same contract, same missing functionality, same impact.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Recovery queuedPayloadHashes array grows unboundedly with no cleanup mechanism, enabling storage bloat DoS by compromised recovery signers
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All four threats describe unbounded push-only growth of queuedPayloadHashes with no deletion or size limit. Same function (Recovery.queuePayload), same storage bloat mechanism. Note: TECH2-H007 here refers to the 'Recovery queue storage grows unbounded' variant (not the weight overflow variant).

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: ERC4337v07.validateUserOp performs ETH deposit to EntryPoint via depositTo before signature validation, enabling griefing/forced fund movement and potential reentrancy
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All five threats target the same code ordering issue: depositTo{value: missingAccountFunds} precedes isValidSignature in validateUserOp. Whether framed as griefing, forced deposit, or reentrancy, the root cause is identical external value transfer before auth. Note: TECH2-H004 here refers to the 'ERC-4337 validateUserOp can be reentered during depositTo' variant (not the gas 63/64 variant).

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Guest contract fallback executes arbitrary calls without authentication, allowing anyone to drain ETH or tokens accidentally sent to the Guest singleton address
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All six threats describe the same vulnerability: Guest.fallback decodes packed calls from msg.data and executes via LibOptim.call without any authentication, enabling anyone to drain funds. Same contract, same function, same unauthenticated execution mechanism.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: LibBytes out-of-bounds reads in Payload.fromPackedCalls and other non-PermissionValidator contexts return zero-padded values silently, enabling malformed payload/signature misparse
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All four threats describe LibBytes missing bounds checks causing silent zero-fill on OOB reads in payload parsing and signature recovery contexts (distinct from the PermissionValidator-specific bypass in DUP-001). Same library defect, overlapping consumer functions. Note: TECH2-H006 here refers to the 'Out-of-bounds reads in Payload.fromPackedCalls' variant (not the Recovery 65-byte signature variant).

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Cumulative permission usage limits never persisted to storage due to Solidity memory struct copy semantics in PermissionValidator.validatePermission, allowing unlimited spending across transactions
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All three threats describe the same memory semantics bug: validatePermission copies usageLimit struct to array then modifies the local copy, leaving the array element at 0. ECON2-H011 describes intra-payload non-propagation which is the same root cause (same struct copy issue in same function). Same code defect, same impact of defeating cumulative limits.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Gas limit check in Calls._execute does not account for EVM 63/64 rule, allowing relayers to provide just enough gas to pass the check but not enough for the actual sub-call
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: Both threats describe the same vulnerability: gasleft() < gasLimit check passes but LibOptim.call only forwards 63/64 of gas, causing silent failure when combined with BEHAVIOR_IGNORE_ERROR. Same function (Calls._execute), same mechanism. Note: TECH2-H004 here refers to the 'Gas Limit Check Bypass via EVM 63/64 Rule' variant.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Session usage limits consumed permanently even when subsequent BEHAVIOR_IGNORE_ERROR calls fail, enabling griefing of session key allocations
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: Both threats describe the same issue: incrementUsageLimit succeeds as the first call, but subsequent operational calls with IGNORE_ERROR can fail, permanently consuming usage limits without achieving intended actions. Same mechanism, same impact.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Calls.selfExecute does not consume Sequence nonces, creating potential replay or nonce accounting gaps when used via ERC4337 or other self-call paths
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: Both threats target the same code defect: selfExecute decodes a payload via fromPackedCalls but never calls _consumeNonce, meaning nonce fields are ignored. TECH-H012 focuses on static signature replay risk via this path; ECON2-H014 focuses on nonce accounting gap via ERC4337. Same root cause function (Calls.selfExecute missing _consumeNonce).
